course_title: OCP Diablo 400: Disaggregated Power for High-Density AI Racks
course_subtitle: A practical Academy course on the Diablo 400 v0.7.0 base specification, with optional future-work context on facility LVDC power distribution.
terminology_notes: For spoken narration, refer to the Diablo bipolar output as plus-minus 400 volts DC when the course is discussing the standard plus-minus 400 volts DC case. Use 800 volts DC only when explicitly discussing pole-to-pole voltage or a monopolar 800 volts DC option.
slide_drafting_notes: Include tooltip-style definitions and/or authoritative reference links for electrical terms that may be unfamiliar to data center and IT technicians. Use _term_references.md as the working glossary source.

sources.title: DCF Power Distribution LVDC White Paper Version 1.0
SemiAnalysis: Inside the 800VDC Revolution - Part 1
sources.usage_note: Used for framing, market context, transition-phase analysis, and future-looking insights. Article text and captured assets are not included in this repository.

sources.title: Microsoft: Mt Diablo - Disaggregated Power Fueling the Next Wave of AI Platforms

sources.usage_note: Comparison begins at 12:17. Three collaborators compare assembly, connectors, cables, and related implementation details across the Meta, Google, and Microsoft Diablo implementations.

modules.title: Why Diablo 400 Exists
modules.subtitle: AI rack density, higher-voltage distribution, and the sidecar pattern.

slides.title: Why Diablo 400 Exists
slides.subtitle: AI rack density, higher-voltage distribution, and the sidecar pattern.

slides.title: Core Path plus Optional Future Work
slides.subtitle: A 35-minute required course, with a 10-15 minute optional LVDC future-work module.

slides.title: AI Rack Power Is Moving Faster Than Legacy Architectures
slides.subtitle: AI systems are turning power delivery into a rack-level architecture constraint.

bullets.text: Cloud racks that once fit below 20 kW are being joined by AI racks in the hundreds of kilowatts.
Accelerators, networking, memory, and liquid cooling compete with power conversion hardware.
Interconnect density, power delivery, and thermal design now move together.
A disaggregated sidecar lets the IT rack focus on compute while the power rack handles conversion and support.

figure_plan.note: Small timeline showing conventional racks, 48 V scaling, Diablo sidecar, and 1 MW-class roadmap. Use article/blog framing only as context, redrawn in Academy style.

figure.alt: Timeline showing rack power density progression toward Diablo 400
figure.caption: AI rack density turns power delivery into an architecture decision.

slides.title: Current, Copper, and I2R Losses
slides.subtitle: Higher voltage reduces current for the same power, which reduces conductor stress.

bullets.text: For a fixed power target, raising voltage lowers current.
I2R loss rises with the square of current, so high current gets expensive quickly.
Designers spend the gain on smaller conductors, simpler routing, and less heat.
Plus-minus 400 volt distribution keeps current, copper, and serviceability within a practical envelope.

figure_plan.note: Two-path comparison: low voltage/high current/heavy copper versus plus-minus 400 volt distribution/lower current/practical cabling.

figure.alt: Silent animation showing that higher distribution voltage lowers current and I2R loss pressure for the same power target.
figure.caption: At fixed power, higher distribution voltage lowers current and reduces conductor burden.
figure.svg:
<svg viewBox="0 0 720 320" role="img" aria-label="Current and conductor comparison" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg">
  <defs><marker id="arrow-current" viewBox="0 0 10 10" refX="8" refY="5" markerWidth="6" markerHeight="6" orient="auto"><path d="M0 0 L10 5 L0 10 Z" fill="#6FA030"/></marker><style>.t{font-family:Open Sans,Arial,sans-serif;fill:#5F6062}.h{font-weight:800;font-size:20px}.s{font-size:14px}.label{font-size:12px;font-weight:700;letter-spacing:.08em;text-transform:uppercase}.box{fill:#fff;stroke:#d1d5db;stroke-width:2}.focus{fill:#f5fbef;stroke:#8DC63F;stroke-width:3}</style></defs>
  <text class="t label" x="160" y="34" text-anchor="middle">Lower voltage path</text><text class="t label" x="560" y="34" text-anchor="middle">Higher voltage path</text>
  <rect class="box" x="55" y="58" width="250" height="205" rx="12"/><rect class="focus" x="415" y="58" width="250" height="205" rx="12"/>

  <text class="t h" x="540" y="95" text-anchor="middle">Lower current</text><text class="t s" x="540" y="122" text-anchor="middle">Practical cabling and routing</text>
  <rect x="95" y="166" width="170" height="36" rx="18" fill="#5F6062"/><rect x="116" y="174" width="128" height="20" rx="10" fill="#9ca3af"/><text class="t s" x="180" y="232" text-anchor="middle">I2R loss rises with current squared</text>
  <rect x="470" y="174" width="140" height="20" rx="10" fill="#8DC63F"/><rect x="495" y="181" width="90" height="6" rx="3" fill="#ffffff" opacity="0.75"/><text class="t s" x="540" y="232" text-anchor="middle">Plus-minus 400 volt distribution</text>

slides.title: From In-Rack Power to the Sidecar Model

source_refs.source: OCP DCF Power Distribution LVDC white paper v1.0

slides.subtitle: The key move is physical disaggregation: power beside the IT rack, not inside it.

bullets.text: Receives three-phase AC and delivers controlled high-voltage DC toward nearby IT racks.
Preserves space for accelerators, switches, manifolds, and cooling hardware.
Places conversion, distribution, protection, and optional storage in a rack optimized for power work.
A common interface helps suppliers build toward the same handoff point.

figure_plan.note: Academy-style sidecar block diagram: facility AC input -> Diablo power rack -> plus-minus 400 volts DC output -> IT rack. Use spec Figure 1 and Google sidecar animation only as reference.

figure.alt: Diablo sidecar power rack feeding an IT rack
figure.caption: The sidecar moves conversion, protection, and optional energy storage beside the compute rack.
figure.svg:
<svg viewBox="0 0 760 360" role="img" aria-label="Diablo sidecar block diagram" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg">
  <defs><marker id="arrow-sidecar" viewBox="0 0 10 10" refX="8" refY="5" markerWidth="7" markerHeight="7" orient="auto"><path d="M0 0 L10 5 L0 10 Z" fill="#6FA030"/></marker><style>.t{font-family:Open Sans,Arial,sans-serif;fill:#5F6062}.h{font-weight:800;font-size:18px}.s{font-size:13px}.tiny{font-size:11px}.rack{fill:#fff;stroke:#d1d5db;stroke-width:2}.power{fill:#f5fbef;stroke:#8DC63F;stroke-width:3}.unit{fill:#ffffff;stroke:#8DC63F;stroke-width:1.5}</style></defs>
  <rect class="rack" x="30" y="108" width="140" height="120" rx="12"/><text class="t h" x="100" y="146" text-anchor="middle">Facility AC</text><text class="t s" x="100" y="174" text-anchor="middle">3-phase input</text>
  <rect class="power" x="245" y="36" width="270" height="288" rx="14"/><text class="t h" x="380" y="68" text-anchor="middle">Diablo power rack</text><rect class="unit" x="285" y="92" width="190" height="38" rx="7"/><text class="t s" x="380" y="116" text-anchor="middle">AC PDU</text><rect class="unit" x="285" y="142" width="190" height="38" rx="7"/><text class="t s" x="380" y="166" text-anchor="middle">AC-DC PSU shelves</text><rect class="unit" x="285" y="192" width="190" height="38" rx="7"/><text class="t s" x="380" y="216" text-anchor="middle">DC PDU and protection</text><rect class="unit" x="285" y="242" width="90" height="34" rx="7"/><text class="t tiny" x="330" y="264" text-anchor="middle">BBU</text><rect class="unit" x="385" y="242" width="90" height="34" rx="7"/><text class="t tiny" x="430" y="264" text-anchor="middle">CBU</text>
  <rect class="rack" x="590" y="96" width="140" height="144" rx="12"/><text class="t h" x="660" y="135" text-anchor="middle">IT rack</text><text class="t s" x="660" y="164" text-anchor="middle">xPUs</text><text class="t s" x="660" y="184" text-anchor="middle">networking</text><text class="t s" x="660" y="204" text-anchor="middle">cooling</text>
  <line x1="170" y1="168" x2="238" y2="168" stroke="#6FA030" stroke-width="4" marker-end="url(#arrow-sidecar)"/><line x1="515" y1="168" x2="583" y2="168" stroke="#6FA030" stroke-width="4" marker-end="url(#arrow-sidecar)"/><text class="t s" x="550" y="152" text-anchor="middle">plus-minus 400 volts DC</text>
  <text class="t s" x="380" y="342" text-anchor="middle">Conversion, protection, and optional storage move beside the compute rack.</text>
</svg>

slides.title: OCP Tenets and the Rack & Power Home
slides.subtitle: Diablo 400 is a shared infrastructure layer, not a one-vendor design pattern.

bullets.text: Defines high-voltage interfaces and mating expectations the community can refine together.
Uses higher-voltage distribution to reduce current and conductor losses.
Targets hundreds of kilowatts and power-rack reuse across IT generations.
Separates longer-lived power infrastructure from faster-moving compute platforms.
Learners should follow future work through the OCP Rack & Power project.

slides.title: Check Your Power Architecture Mental Model
slides.subtitle: Submit each question once to continue. Correct answers are not required, and retry is available.

questions.prompt: Why does the Diablo 400 architecture move major power conversion and support functions into a sidecar rack?

choices.text: To preserve IT rack space and make conversion, protection, distribution, and optional storage serviceable as power infrastructure.
To eliminate the need for three-phase AC input power at the facility level.
To make every compute tray, accelerator, and liquid-cooling manifold identical across vendors.
To remove the need for grounding, protection, and service access controls.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. The sidecar pattern disaggregates power so the IT rack can prioritize compute, networking, and cooling while power hardware is serviced in a purpose-built rack.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Review the sidecar model. The important move is physical disaggregation: power conversion and support functions sit beside the IT rack, not inside it.

questions.prompt: Which challenges does higher-voltage distribution help manage in high-power AI racks?

choices.text: Lower current for a given power target
Reduced conductor heating and I2R losses
More practical copper, routing, and serviceability tradeoffs
Removal of all shock and arc hazards
No need for a shared ecosystem interface

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. Higher voltage helps manage current, copper, heat, and practical service constraints, but it does not remove electrical hazards.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Look back at the physics slide. Higher voltage helps with current and I2R losses, but protection, grounding, and service practices still matter.

slides.thank_you_message: Thank you for completing Module 1: Why Diablo 400 Exists.
summary_items: AI rack density is pushing power into hundreds of kilowatts and toward 1 MW-class infrastructure.
Higher voltage helps control current, copper, heat, and serviceability constraints.
The sidecar model moves conversion, distribution, protection, and optional energy storage out of the IT rack.
OCP standardization gives the ecosystem a common power interface to build around.

slides_outline: Title: Why Diablo 400 Exists
Course Overview: Core Path plus Optional Future Work

From In-Rack Power to the Sidecar Model

Key Takeaways / Up Next

modules.title: Diablo 400 Architecture and Power Path
modules.subtitle: Rack building blocks, AC input, DC output, dynamic loading, and efficiency.

slides.title: Diablo 400 Architecture and Power Path
slides.subtitle: Rack building blocks, AC input, DC output, dynamic loading, and efficiency.

slides.title: Learning Objectives
slides.subtitle: By the end of this module, you will be able to:
objectives: Trace the Diablo 400 power path from AC input to DC output.
Describe the sidecar physical envelope and serviceability assumptions.
Distinguish holdup, ride-through, BBU, and CBU functions.
Recognize the core output, dynamic loading, and efficiency requirements.

slides.title: Scope and Physical Envelope
slides.subtitle: The sidecar must be movable, serviceable, and structurally ready for dense power hardware.
rows: 2329 mm height baseline; 600 to 711 mm width; 1200 to 1219.2 mm depth
Fits the sidecar beside high-density IT racks while allowing implementation variation
Supports shelves, busbars, storage, cables, and protection modules
Sliding shelves, blind-mate connectors, static or sliding rails
Large power blocks can be pushed in, pulled out, and serviced
Doors, optional locks, intrusion detection, emergency shutoff
Power hardware is treated as a field-serviceable system

figure_plan.note: Use table only. The numbers are more important than a rack rendering on this slide.

slides.title: AC Input and Power Quality
slides.subtitle: The sidecar must be a good AC load upstream while feeding DC conversion downstream.
rows: Single 3-phase AC feed or dual feeds for N+N redundancy
Availability can be built into the AC input path
Regional facility power options are anticipated
Delta service is also in scope
PF >0.95 below 20 percent load; PF >0.99 at 20 percent and above; THD tightens with load
The rack must behave well electrically upstream

slides.title: Ride-Through, BBU, CBU, and DC Output
slides.subtitle: Diablo 400 separates short-duration holdup, event ride-through, and optional storage.

bullets.text: Built-in supply energy that keeps output alive during the first moment after AC loss.
Continued operation through defined voltage dropouts, sags, swells, and overvoltage events.
Optional storage blocks support longer backup or transient buffering when the design uses them.
A BBU option must support 45 to 90 seconds at 100 percent loading.
Nominal plus-minus 400 volts DC, with common as the midpoint between plus and minus poles.

figure_plan.note: Compact output stack showing +400 volts DC, Common/Midpoint/Return, and -400 volts DC, plus a side rail for holdup -> storage ramp.

figure.alt: Silent animation showing built-in holdup, optional BBU or CBU support, and the plus-minus 400 volt DC output model.
figure.caption: Holdup bridges the first moment after AC loss; optional storage can then support the plus-minus 400 volt DC output.

slides.title: Dynamic Loading and End-to-End Efficiency
slides.subtitle: The power rack has to support AI transient behavior, not only steady-state load.
rows: Output must recover quickly after step changes
50, 90, and 140 percent steps from 10 percent minimum load
Large AI load movement is part of the test picture
Current can change quickly enough to stress controls and protection
Voltage excursions are bounded during dynamic events
Up to 1.1 MW depending on AC input configuration
Architecture is sized for very high rack-class power
>95 percent at 20 percent load; up to >98 percent at 50 percent load for 480 V AC input
Losses are measured end-to-end from AC PDU to DC PDU

figure_plan.note: Small transient waveform with step load, bounded overshoot/undershoot, and 3 ms settling annotation.

slides.title: Check Your Diablo 400 Power Path
slides.subtitle: Submit each question once to continue. Correct answers are not required, and retry is available.

questions.prompt: What does the plus-minus 400 volt output model describe in this course?

choices.text: A bipolar DC output with plus and minus poles around a common midpoint.
A single unipolar 400 volt AC feed from the facility switchgear.
A signal connector used only for telemetry and firmware updates.
A battery-only output that bypasses the power shelves.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. Diablo 400 uses a bipolar DC output model where common is the midpoint between the plus and minus poles.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Review the DC output model. The course uses plus-minus 400 volt to describe a bipolar DC output with a common midpoint.

questions.prompt: Which items are part of the dynamic loading and efficiency picture in Diablo 400?

choices.text: 3 ms transient settling time
50, 90, and 140 percent load steps from 10 percent minimum load
1 amp per microsecond slew rate
Unlimited voltage overshoot during AI load changes
Efficiency measured only inside the IT tray, not end to end

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. The spec treats transient behavior and end-to-end efficiency as part of the power-rack requirement set.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Revisit the dynamic loading table. The named requirements include fast settling, defined step loads, and a current slew-rate expectation.

slides.thank_you_message: Thank you for completing Module 2: Diablo 400 Architecture and Power Path.
summary_items: The sidecar is a serviceable AC-to-DC power rack built for high-density deployment.
Input requirements cover feed redundancy, voltage choices, frequency, power factor, and THD.
Holdup, ride-through, BBU, and CBU are related but distinct support mechanisms.
Dynamic loading and end-to-end efficiency are central Diablo 400 requirements.

slides_outline: Title: Architecture and Power Path
Learning Objectives
Scope and Physical Envelope
AC Input and Power Quality
Ride-Through, BBU, CBU, and DC Output
Dynamic Loading and End-to-End Efficiency
Key Takeaways / Up Next

modules.title: Interfaces, Safety, Telemetry, and Adoption
modules.subtitle: Output protection, interconnects, grounding, telemetry, compliance, and OCP participation.

slides.title: Interfaces, Safety, Telemetry, and Adoption
slides.subtitle: Output protection, interconnects, grounding, telemetry, compliance, and OCP participation.

slides.title: Learning Objectives
slides.subtitle: By the end of this module, you will be able to:
objectives: Explain how the AC PDU, DC PDU, PSU shelves, busbar, and cables work together.
Compare high resistance midpoint grounding with solid midpoint grounding.
Identify management, telemetry, and environmental operation expectations.
Name the major safety, EMC, energy-storage, and environmental compliance categories.

slides.title: AC PDU, DC PDU, PSU Shelves, and Cables
slides.subtitle: The interface layer turns the electrical architecture into serviceable rack hardware.

bullets.text: Accepts up to twelve 200 A cords, includes protection fuses, and feeds the power shelves.
Up to 2 OU, front-accessible, hot-swappable, hot-pluggable, and galvanically isolated.
Supports output protection, telemetry, on-off control, and no-voltage contact behavior.
50 kW and 100 kW options with protection elements and service-safe disconnect behavior.
Single-zone three-conductor distribution for plus, minus, and common, up to 1.1 MW average load power.

resource_callout.title: Implementation comparison
resource_callout.text: For a deeper comparison of the Meta, Google, and Microsoft Diablo implementations, jump to 12:17 in the OCP Diablo 400 project video. The presenters compare assembly, connectors, cables, and related implementation details.

figure_plan.note: Rack block diagram: overhead AC busway -> AC PDU -> PSU shelves -> vertical busbar -> DC PDU -> IT rack cables.

figure.alt: Silent animation tracing the serviceable interface path from overhead AC busway through the Diablo power rack to the IT rack.
figure.caption: The path converts, distributes, protects, monitors, and disconnects at deliberate service boundaries.

  <rect class="block" x="678" y="92" width="58" height="70" rx="10"/><text class="t s" x="707" y="121" text-anchor="middle">IT</text><text class="t s" x="707" y="141" text-anchor="middle">rack</text>
  <line x1="128" y1="127" x2="152" y2="127" stroke="#6FA030" stroke-width="4" marker-end="url(#arrow-rack)"/><line x1="262" y1="127" x2="286" y2="127" stroke="#6FA030" stroke-width="4" marker-end="url(#arrow-rack)"/><line x1="412" y1="127" x2="436" y2="127" stroke="#6FA030" stroke-width="4" marker-end="url(#arrow-rack)"/><line x1="514" y1="127" x2="538" y2="127" stroke="#6FA030" stroke-width="4" marker-end="url(#arrow-rack)"/><line x1="648" y1="127" x2="672" y2="127" stroke="#6FA030" stroke-width="4" marker-end="url(#arrow-rack)"/>
</svg>

slides.title: HRG and Solid Midpoint Grounding
slides.subtitle: Grounding is a design choice that changes fault behavior, service practice, and compatibility.

cards.title: High resistance midpoint grounding
cards.body: Midpoint connects to protective earth through a design-dependent high resistance path. First faults may not interrupt operation, so detection and response matter.

cards.title: Solid midpoint grounding
cards.body: Midpoint connects to protective earth through a solid contact. It is simpler to design and maintain, but fault current and bonding behavior must be handled carefully.

cards.title: Common tradeoff
cards.body: Neither choice removes shock risk between plus 400 and minus 400 volts. Both require clear service and protection assumptions.

cards.title: Compatibility cue
cards.body: Grounding strategy affects vendor interoperability, fault detection, troubleshooting, and multi-rack behavior.

figure_plan.note: Optional mini comparison of midpoint-to-PE paths beside the card grid if space allows; otherwise use cards plus glossary chips.

slides.title: Management, Telemetry, and Environmental Operation
slides.subtitle: The spec leaves room for implementation choices, but names the interfaces operators should expect.

bullets.text: May interface with all Diablo 400 building blocks inside the sidecar.
Redfish is recommended; proprietary interfaces are allowed when needed.
Ethernet, I2C, PMBus, Modbus, and CAN bus are listed interface options.
Sidecar-to-data-center, sidecar-to-sidecar, sidecar-to-IT-rack, and internal building-block links may all matter.
Fully air-cooled, front-to-back airflow, preferred -5 C to 45 C ambient, and up to 3050 m without derating.

slides.title: Safety, EMC, Sustainability, and Implementation Boundaries
slides.subtitle: These requirements are what make the shared architecture buildable and inspectable.

bullets.text: Integrated rack and subcomponents must comply with applicable IEC/UL 62368-1 requirements and national deviations.
AC mains-connected assemblies must withstand 10 kA to 40 kA available short-circuit fault current, depending on application.
Live parts above PS2 limits must not be accessible without interlock, switch, or de-energization method.
Creepage, clearance, overcurrent protection, PE bonding, leakage current, and ground-fault detection all require review.
Battery standards, NFPA 855, CISPR 32/35, emissions, surge, ESD, and environmental regulations are in scope.

slides.title: Check Your Interface and Safety Boundaries
slides.subtitle: Submit each question once to continue. Correct answers are not required, and retry is available.

questions.prompt: Why does the grounding choice matter for Diablo 400 implementations?

choices.text: It changes fault behavior, detection and response assumptions, service practice, and compatibility expectations.
It turns the DC output into a low-voltage AC distribution system.
It removes the need to review shock risk between plus and minus poles.
It replaces the need for output protection and disconnect behavior.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. HRG and solid midpoint grounding are design choices with different fault and service implications.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Review the grounding slide. The choice affects fault behavior and operations; it does not eliminate protection or shock-risk analysis.

questions.prompt: Which topics belong in the shared interface, telemetry, safety, or adoption boundary for Diablo 400?

choices.text: Redfish or other management interfaces for sidecar telemetry
AC PDU, power shelf, DC PDU, and output cable behavior
Safety, EMC, environmental, and regulatory compliance review
A requirement that every vendor use the same proprietary management protocol
Ignoring implementation variation across adopters

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. Diablo 400 standardizes the interface picture while leaving room for implementation choices.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Look back across Module 3. It connects serviceable hardware, telemetry paths, safety boundaries, EMC, and ecosystem adoption.

slides.next_module_title: Optional: Future Work - Facility LVDC Power Distribution
slides.thank_you_message: Thank you for completing Module 3: Interfaces, Safety, Telemetry, and Adoption.
summary_items: The required core now covers the Diablo 400 motivation, architecture, interfaces, grounding, telemetry, safety, EMC, and compliance boundaries.
Implementation teams should audit the specification tables carefully before design or deployment decisions.
The optional module looks beyond the rack sidecar to facility-level LVDC future work.

slides.subtitle: By the end of this optional module, you will be able to:
objectives: Distinguish rack-level Diablo 400 from facility-level LVDC distribution.
Explain the staged LVDC adoption view from IT racks to broader facility loads.
Recognize why voltage classes, bands, polarity, and earthing choices matter.
Name future-work areas around protection, conversion, energy storage, grid interaction, arc flash, and standards.

slides.title: From Sidecar to Facility Distribution

slides.caption: The optional module reframes Diablo 400 as the near-term rack-side interface inside a longer facility-level DC roadmap.

figure_plan.note: Three-stage facility roadmap: Stage 1 IT racks plus optional cooling, Stage 2 larger IT module, Stage 3 fuller LVDC distribution between MV input and data center equipment.

figure.alt: Three-stage roadmap from sidecar to facility LVDC distribution
figure.caption: The LVDC white paper frames Diablo as a practical first step toward broader DC distribution.
figure.svg:

  <defs><marker id="arrow-stage" viewBox="0 0 10 10" refX="8" refY="5" markerWidth="7" markerHeight="7" orient="auto"><path d="M0 0 L10 5 L0 10 Z" fill="#6FA030"/></marker><style>.t{font-family:Open Sans,Arial,sans-serif;fill:#5F6062}.h{font-weight:800;font-size:18px}.s{font-size:13px}.tiny{font-size:11px}.stage{fill:#fff;stroke:#d1d5db;stroke-width:2}.focus{fill:#f5fbef;stroke:#8DC63F;stroke-width:3}</style></defs>
  <text class="t h" x="380" y="34" text-anchor="middle">From rack sidecar to facility LVDC</text>
  <rect class="focus" x="42" y="82" width="190" height="190" rx="14"/><text class="t h" x="137" y="118" text-anchor="middle">Stage 1</text><text class="t s" x="137" y="150" text-anchor="middle">DC to IT racks</text><text class="t tiny" x="137" y="176" text-anchor="middle">optional cooling support</text><rect x="86" y="204" width="102" height="38" rx="7" fill="#fff" stroke="#8DC63F"/><text class="t tiny" x="137" y="228" text-anchor="middle">Diablo sidecar</text>
  <rect class="stage" x="286" y="82" width="190" height="190" rx="14"/><text class="t h" x="381" y="118" text-anchor="middle">Stage 2</text><text class="t s" x="381" y="150" text-anchor="middle">larger IT module</text><text class="t tiny" x="381" y="176" text-anchor="middle">IT plus associated cooling</text><rect x="330" y="204" width="102" height="38" rx="7" fill="#fff" stroke="#d1d5db"/><text class="t tiny" x="381" y="228" text-anchor="middle">module bus</text>
  <rect class="stage" x="530" y="82" width="190" height="190" rx="14"/><text class="t h" x="625" y="118" text-anchor="middle">Stage 3</text><text class="t s" x="625" y="150" text-anchor="middle">fuller LVDC</text><text class="t tiny" x="625" y="176" text-anchor="middle">from MV input to loads</text><rect x="574" y="204" width="102" height="38" rx="7" fill="#fff" stroke="#d1d5db"/><text class="t tiny" x="625" y="228" text-anchor="middle">facility DC</text>
  <line x1="236" y1="177" x2="280" y2="177" stroke="#6FA030" stroke-width="4" marker-end="url(#arrow-stage)"/><line x1="480" y1="177" x2="524" y2="177" stroke="#6FA030" stroke-width="4" marker-end="url(#arrow-stage)"/>

</svg>

slides.subtitle: A future LVDC ecosystem needs shared voltage language, not just a headline voltage.

cards.title: LVDC boundary
cards.body: The white paper limits this discussion to systems at or below 1500 volts DC.

cards.title: Voltage bands
cards.body: Nominal, rated, and operating voltage are different ideas; DC systems may use narrow or wide operating bands.

cards.title: Bipolar vs unipolar
cards.body: Bipolar systems can reduce voltage to earth for a given pole-to-pole transfer, but add midpoint and protection choices.

cards.title: TN-S grounding
cards.body: Neutral and protective-earth functions are carried separately. Useful contrast point for solidly grounded designs.

cards.title: IT grounding
cards.body: Source is isolated from earth or connected through high impedance, with exposed conductive parts earthed.

figure_plan.note: If the 5-card grid feels crowded, convert to a two-row diagram: voltage families on top and topology/earthing choices below.

slides.title: Protection, Conversion, ESS, and Grid Interaction

source_refs.source: OCP DCF Power Distribution LVDC white paper v1.0

OCP DCF Power Distribution LVDC white paper v1.0
OCP DCF Power Distribution LVDC white paper v1.0

slides.subtitle: Future LVDC systems are coordinated power-electronic systems, not passive wiring upgrades.

bullets.text: Low inductance and high capacitance can make DC fault current rise on microsecond to millisecond timescales.
Selectivity and fast clearing must protect people and equipment while keeping healthy loads online.
Semiconductor breakers can interrupt very quickly, but portfolios, losses, and deployment models are still maturing.
Rectifiers, interlink converters, and solid-state transformers all shape where AC becomes DC.
Storage can support load smoothing, ride-through, backup, and grid response when coordinated carefully.

figure_plan.note: Layered system sketch: MV/AC source -> conversion/SST -> DC bus -> protection -> IT/cooling loads, with ESS tied to the bus.

figure.alt: Silent conceptual animation of coordinated LVDC conversion, storage support, selective protection, and healthy-load continuity.
figure.caption: Conversion, storage, and protection respond together while healthy loads remain online.
figure.svg:
<svg viewBox="0 0 760 360" role="img" aria-label="Future LVDC system components" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg">
  <defs><marker id="arrow-lvdc" viewBox="0 0 10 10" refX="8" refY="5" markerWidth="7" markerHeight="7" orient="auto"><path d="M0 0 L10 5 L0 10 Z" fill="#6FA030"/></marker><style>.t{font-family:Open Sans,Arial,sans-serif;fill:#5F6062}.h{font-weight:800;font-size:17px}.s{font-size:12px}.block{fill:#fff;stroke:#d1d5db;stroke-width:2}.focus{fill:#f5fbef;stroke:#8DC63F;stroke-width:2.5}</style></defs>
  <text class="t h" x="380" y="34" text-anchor="middle">Coordinated LVDC power-electronic system</text>
  <rect class="block" x="34" y="110" width="105" height="76" rx="10"/><text class="t h" x="86" y="143" text-anchor="middle">MV/AC</text><text class="t s" x="86" y="164" text-anchor="middle">source</text>
  <rect class="focus" x="177" y="95" width="122" height="106" rx="10"/><text class="t h" x="238" y="131" text-anchor="middle">Conversion</text><text class="t s" x="238" y="153" text-anchor="middle">rectifier</text><text class="t s" x="238" y="173" text-anchor="middle">or SST</text>
  <rect class="block" x="337" y="110" width="105" height="76" rx="10"/><text class="t h" x="389" y="142" text-anchor="middle">DC bus</text><text class="t s" x="389" y="164" text-anchor="middle">LVDC</text>
  <rect class="focus" x="480" y="95" width="122" height="106" rx="10"/><text class="t h" x="541" y="129" text-anchor="middle">Protection</text><text class="t s" x="541" y="153" text-anchor="middle">selective</text><text class="t s" x="541" y="173" text-anchor="middle">fast clearing</text>
  <rect class="block" x="640" y="84" width="86" height="58" rx="10"/><text class="t s" x="683" y="118" text-anchor="middle">IT loads</text><rect class="block" x="640" y="164" width="86" height="58" rx="10"/><text class="t s" x="683" y="198" text-anchor="middle">Cooling</text>
  <rect class="focus" x="334" y="244" width="112" height="58" rx="10"/><text class="t h" x="390" y="270" text-anchor="middle">ESS</text><text class="t s" x="390" y="290" text-anchor="middle">storage</text>
  <line x1="139" y1="148" x2="171" y2="148" stroke="#6FA030" stroke-width="4" marker-end="url(#arrow-lvdc)"/><line x1="299" y1="148" x2="331" y2="148" stroke="#6FA030" stroke-width="4" marker-end="url(#arrow-lvdc)"/><line x1="442" y1="148" x2="474" y2="148" stroke="#6FA030" stroke-width="4" marker-end="url(#arrow-lvdc)"/><line x1="602" y1="148" x2="634" y2="113" stroke="#6FA030" stroke-width="3" marker-end="url(#arrow-lvdc)"/><line x1="602" y1="148" x2="634" y2="193" stroke="#6FA030" stroke-width="3" marker-end="url(#arrow-lvdc)"/><line x1="390" y1="244" x2="390" y2="192" stroke="#6FA030" stroke-width="4" marker-end="url(#arrow-lvdc)"/>
  <text class="t s" x="380" y="334" text-anchor="middle">Fast faults, storage, conversion, and control must be designed as a system.</text>
</svg>

slides.title: Standards Gaps and OCP Future Work

source_refs.source: OCP DCF Power Distribution LVDC white paper v1.0
OCP DCF Power Distribution LVDC white paper v1.0
OCP DCF Power Distribution LVDC white paper v1.0

OCP DCF Power Distribution LVDC white paper v1.0
OCP DCF Power Distribution LVDC white paper v1.0

slides.subtitle: The white paper treats standards work as a system-level agenda, not a paperwork exercise.

bullets.text: Architecture, controls, load sharing, fault response, maintenance, and worker protection need clearer data-center guidance.
Existing methods and practices are more mature for AC; DC-specific research and validation are needed.
Use existing standards where they fit, work with certifying bodies, and apply hazard-based risk assessment.
Laboratory testing, modeling, and hardware-in-the-loop simulation can de-risk controls before scale deployment.
DCF Power Distribution and Rack & Power need aligned handoffs from facility distribution to rack power.

slides.title: Check Your LVDC Future-Work Lens
slides.subtitle: Submit each question once to continue. Correct answers are not required, and retry is available.

questions.prompt: Why does the LVDC future-work discussion distinguish nominal, rated, and operating voltage?

choices.text: Because future DC systems need shared voltage language for architecture, protection, certification, and interoperability.
Because every DC system must operate at exactly one fixed voltage with no operating band.
Because voltage terminology replaces the need for grounding and protection choices.
Because nominal voltage is only a marketing term with no engineering value.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. The white paper frames voltage language as part of making LVDC systems designable, protectable, and interoperable.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Review the voltage-bands slide. Nominal, rated, and operating voltage are different concepts that matter for system design and standards.

questions.prompt: Which future-work areas need coordinated system-level treatment for facility LVDC?

choices.text: Fast DC protection and selectivity
DC arc flash research and worker-protection guidance
Controls, load sharing, fault response, and validation methods
Waiting to use any existing standards until a perfect LVDC standard exists
Treating future LVDC as passive wiring with no power-electronics coordination

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. The future-work agenda is about coordinated architecture, protection, controls, standards, and validation.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Review the standards and future-work slide. The course points to interim use of existing standards plus targeted LVDC research and validation.

motion_intro.title: Future Work - Facility LVDC Power Distribution
motion_intro.subtitle: How sidecar power connects to broader LVDC architectures, protection, standards, and future OCP work.

term_glossary.tooltip: Heat produced when current flows through resistance. Because current is squared, lower current can sharply reduce conductor heating.
Ability of equipment to keep operating through defined voltage or frequency disturbances instead of tripping immediately.
Short-duration stored energy that keeps the supply output alive long enough for another support path to engage after input loss.
Battery backup unit. In Diablo 400, an optional energy-storage block that can support 45 to 90 seconds at full load when used.
Capacitor bank unit. In Diablo 400, an optional capacitive energy-storage block for short-duration support and transient buffering.
Ratio of active power to apparent power under periodic conditions. Higher values mean the AC input current is being used more effectively.
Total harmonic distortion is a measure of harmonic content in a waveform, used here as an AC input power-quality limit.
Earthing arrangement where neutral and protective-earth functions are carried by separate conductors.
Earthing arrangement where the source is isolated from earth or connected through high impedance, with exposed conductive parts earthed.
Protective conductor path used to bond exposed conductive parts and provide a safer fault-current return path.
Property of a circuit where changing current stores magnetic energy and induces voltage that resists the current change.
Protection coordination goal where the device closest to a fault opens first, keeping unaffected equipment online.
Hazard from release of energy caused by an electric arc; it can injure people and damage equipment.
Short-circuit current rating. The maximum prospective short-circuit current an assembly is rated to withstand.
Clearance is spacing through air; creepage is spacing along an insulating surface. Both help prevent breakdown or tracking.
Electromagnetic compatibility. Ability of equipment to operate in its electromagnetic environment without causing unacceptable disturbance to other equipment.
DMTF standard for secure, interoperable management of modern IT infrastructure using web-based APIs.
Breaker using semiconductor switching devices to interrupt current much faster than conventional mechanical breakers.
Megawatt-scale power electronic converter intended to replace conventional line-frequency transformer and rectifier units in some architectures.
Low voltage direct current. In this course, the OCP LVDC discussion is limited to systems at or below 1500 volts DC.

figure_plan_summary.approach: Use inline SVG technical diagrams and tables rather than AI-generated bitmap art. Source figures are used as references or audit anchors; no SemiAnalysis wording or visuals are copied verbatim.
figure_plan_summary.tooltip_style: Show glossary chips near the slide content. Each chip has a short definition and an optional Learn more link to OCP, IEC, DMTF, UL, NERC/IEEE, or NFPA references.
